ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists specifically for Korean spicy rice cake soup (tteokbokki gukmul). The closest defensible FoodOn anchor is the generic rice cake food product class. A more specific Korean soup class is not available in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for Korean spicy rice cake soup. A00LC (rice-based dishes) is the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor. Annotated as closest_parent match.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for Korean spicy rice cake soup as a specific food concept.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of Korean spicy rice cake soup is approximately 350 g, containing a mix of rice cake pieces, fish cake slices, and spicy broth.

nutrient_source.title: Korean Spicy Rice Cake Soup — Singapore HPB Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Korean spicy rice cake soup (cr_id F05100072). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this record at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central analogous entry for tteokbokki (USDA SR Legacy) as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown; this is documented as an adapted value.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — rice cake GI reference
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Korean spicy rice cake soup (tteokbokki gukmul) in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 72 is estimated from the closest analogous food: plain rice cakes (puffed/glutinous rice cake), which have a published GI of approximately 70–82 in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021. The soup dilution and protein/fibre content (3.63 g protein, 1.89 g fibre per 100 g) modestly attenuate glycaemic response relative to plain rice cake. GI of 72 is used as a conservative mid-range estimate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (72 × 7.46) / 100 ≈ 5.4, rounded to 5.5. Carbohydrate per 350 g serving = 2.13 × 3.5 = 7.455 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 512 mg per 100 g — driven by gochujang, soy sauce, and anchovy broth
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid Korean spicy rice cake soup due to its high sodium content of 512 mg per 100 g; if consumed, portion should be restricted to a small bowl (150–200 g) and broth intake minimised, with no additional soy sauce or condiments added.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Rice cake (tteok) is a high-GI ingredient (GI ~72); carbohydrate density is low at 2.13 g per 100 g in soup form but glycaemic impact rises with portion size
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes may consume Korean spicy rice cake soup in small portions (150–200 g) given the low carbohydrate density of 2.13 g per 100 g in soup form, but should monitor total rice cake intake carefully as tteok is a high-GI food that can elevate postprandial blood glucose when consumed in larger quantities.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium (512 mg per 100 g) raises blood pressure risk; saturated fat is moderate; gochujang-based spice may exacerbate vascular stress
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit Korean spicy rice cake soup due to its high sodium content of 512 mg per 100 g, which contributes to elevated blood pressure and increased cardiovascular risk; consumption should be infrequent, portions kept small, and broth largely avoided.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sodium 512 mg per 100 g exceeds RA threshold; gochujang contains chili (nightshade) which may trigger solanine-related inflammation in RA-sensitive individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or strictly limit Korean spicy rice cake soup due to its high sodium content (512 mg per 100 g) and the presence of gochujang, which contains chili pepper — a nightshade that may exacerbate inflammation in solanine-sensitive RA patients.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Korean Spicy Rice Cake Soup (떡볶이 국물, romanized: Tteokbokki Gukmul) is a Korean dish consisting of chewy cylindrical or flat rice cakes (tteok) simmered in a spicy, savoury broth made from gochujang (Korean fermented chili paste), soy sauce, and anchovy or vegetable stock. The dish typically also contains fish cake slices (eomuk), green onions, and sesame oil, producing a rich, deeply red broth with a bold umami-spice flavour profile. The rice cakes have a soft, chewy, slightly sticky texture that absorbs the spicy broth. Per 100 g, the soup form provides 48 kcal, 3.63 g protein, 2.75 g fat, 2.13 g carbohydrate, 1.89 g dietary fibre, and 512 mg sodium. The low carbohydrate density reflects the soup dilution effect; however, the rice cake component itself is a high-GI food (GI ~72), so glycaemic impact increases with portion size. Sodium at 512 mg per 100 g is clinically significant for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management. The dish is not suitable for low-sodium diets. Tteokbokki is a popular Korean street food and comfort food, widely consumed as a snack or light meal. It is commonly found in Korean restaurants, food courts, and street stalls across Singapore and globally. The dish is not vegetarian or vegan in its traditional form due to fish cake and anchovy broth, and contains gluten from soy sauce and fish cake. It is not suitable for halal diets in its standard preparation unless certified halal ingredients are used.

Is Korean spicy rice cake soup good for weight loss?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F05100072) at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA SR Legacy analogous tteokbokki entry and documented as adapted. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous rice cake entry and documented as analogous estimate. Korean local-language name confirmed from standard Korean culinary terminology.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (0.7 g per 100 g) is an adapted estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy analogous entry for tteokbokki, as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown for this record. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for Korean spicy rice cake soup are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for this specific preparation as of 2026. 3. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate based on plain rice cake GI values from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 (Diabetes Care); no direct GI measurement for tteokbokki gukmul exists in approved sources. 4. The input dietary_tags included 'keto' which is incorrect for a rice cake dish and has been removed. The input also listed 'low_calorie' which is defensible at 48 kcal per 100 g and retained in meal context. 5. Halal status is marked false as standard tteokbokki preparation uses anchovy broth and fish cake which may not be halal-certified; halal-certified versions exist but cannot be assumed for a generic record. 6. The input health_suitability entry for rheumatoid_arthritis referenced 'red meat is rich in arachidonic acid' which is not applicable to this dish and has been removed from the RA caution.
